Title: ‘Surveillance pricing’ ban, RTD overhaul, a ‘super-secret memo’ and more from the Colorado legislature this week
URL: https://www.denverpost.com/2026/05/09/colorado-surveillance-pricing-rtd-overhaul-legislature/
Time Published: 2026-05-09T12:00:27Z
Full Content:
Days after moving to kneecap one potential fall ballot measure, lawmakers are preparing to introduce legislation that would blunt another proposed ballot initiative — one that would give Coloradans the right to buy and sell natural gas. The exact text of the bill has not been released, and it was still being drafted Friday. But the Democratic lawmakers backing it said their proposal would add definitions and “guardrails” to Initiative 177, a two-sentence constitutional amendment that, if placed on the ballot and passed, would give the state’s residents the right to buy natural gas for cooking or heating. It would also give gas producers and utilities the right to sell the product to homes and businesses. “There are a variety of potential implications from a ballot measure that is so poorly defined,” House Speaker Julie McCluskie told reporters, “and it is really our goal to combat any of those dangerous and concerning outcomes if the ballot measure makes it. Now more than ever, it is not the time to play politics with our energy environment and with our energy partners.” Read more What Colorado bills are becoming laws? For the second consecutive year, Colorado lawmakers are set to pass legislation that would ban companies from using price-setting algorithms. And for the second consecutive year, Gov. Jared Polis has greeted the bill with a cold shoulder. The state Senate passed House Bill 1210 on Wednesday on a 19-15 vote, putting the attempt to ban “surveillance pricing” on a glide path to Polis’ desk. If signed into law, the measure would generally prohibit companies from using the mountains of data collected on consumers to set individualized prices on items like groceries, plane tickets and electronics. It would also block companies like Uber or Lyft from using that data to set individualized wages for workers. Read more A Colorado bill that would have allowed the state to invest enterprise fees and other public money in privately managed investment funds died Wednesday in a committee. Sponsors of Senate Bill 180 sought to use the potentially higher returns from private investment funds to pay for low-income childcare programs. However, the proposal faced questions about whether it would ran afoul of the Colorado Constitution’s prohibition on the state owning stocks in corporations. Representatives of Gov. Jared Polis said his office had received a legal opinion from the attorney general’s office giving the OK for this arrangement, but they refused to release it to legislators. Read more Colorado lawmakers have voted down a bill that would’ve required legislative caucuses to disclose their donors, amid ethics investigations into more than a dozen Democratic lawmakers who attended a private retreat and raised an undisclosed amount of money last year. One of those lawmakers, Sen. William Lindstedt, joined with two Republicans Tuesday in voting to kill Senate Bill 168. The bill would have required members of legislative caucuses to regularly file reports describing who donated to the group, how much it raised and what that money was spent on. Lawmakers are required to disclose their own campaign spending and fundraising, but that requirement does not extend to caucuses. Read more State lawmakers killed legislation dubbed the “No Kings Act” on Monday night, with opposition from local government groups tanking a bill that would have allowed Coloradans to sue federal officials who violated their civil rights. Two Democrats -- Sens. Dylan Roberts and Lindsey Daugherty — joined with the Senate Judiciary Committee’s Republican Sens. John Carson and Byron Pelton to kill Senate Bill 176 on a 4-3 vote. Had it passed, the “No Kings Act” would’ve allowed lawsuits against federal officials for violating residents’ civil rights, filling what supporters had described as a gap in state and federal law that largely provides no legal avenue to challenge federal authorities’ actions. The measure was an expanded version of another bill that’s already passed the Senate. That one would allow lawsuits against federal officials involved in immigration enforcement. But Senate Bill 5 is narrower -- meaning that it wouldn’t cover election interference, for instance, or other federal overreach. Read more Coloradans will no longer be able to make 3D-printed guns and agricultural workers will have to work 56 hours in a week before they qualify for overtime under a pair of bills signed into law by Gov. Jared Polis on Monday. House Bill 1144, the ban on 3D-printed guns, goes into effect July 1. The law expands on Colorado’s prior ban on firearms without serial numbers, known as “ghost guns,” by prohibiting the use of 3D printers or computerized milling machines to manufacture firearms or components like large-capacity magazines and rapid-fire trigger activators. Polis had threatened to veto an earlier version of the bill that included a prohibition on selling or distributing “digital instructions” needed to print the firearms or their components. Sponsors stripped that measure out before it reached his desk. Read more Colorado’s embattled Regional Transportation District is set for a sizable overhaul, as state lawmakers prepare to send Gov. Jared Polis a bill that would slash its governing board’s size by 40% while increasing how much those leaders are paid. Senate Bill 150 passed the House Monday on a 39-26 vote. The bill needs a final procedural vote in the Senate before it moves to Polis. The governor is expected to sign the measure into law. The bill is the product of lawmakers’ recent and growing interest in RTD’s governance. SB-150 would trim the district’s currently all-elected board from 15 members to nine, with the new board selected in a hybrid setup. Five of those members would be elected to represent specific districts, while the remaining four would be appointed by the governor. Read more Gov. Jared Polis’ office is pursuing legislation that would allow state agencies to invest enterprise fees and other public money in privately managed investment funds, instead of investing through the Colorado treasurer -- and it’s relying on what a lawmaker called a “super-secret memo” for legal backing. The memo to the governor’s office, which is based on a legal analysis by the attorney general’s office, states that there is legal cover for such a move. But a separate public attorney general opinion states that the “plain text” of the Colorado Constitution “generally prohibits the State from directly owning stock in a corporation.” The memo and legal opinion both come from the attorney general’s office, though the private nature of the memo described by the governor’s office makes it unclear where in the AG’s office the memo came from. Read more Welcome to the last full week of the 2026 session of the Colorado General Assembly. The state’s 100 lawmakers now have 10 days, including today and the weekend, to finish their work for the year. The nearing deadline means the Capitol will be even more abuzz than usual as lawmakers scramble to pass -- or use the clock to kill — legislation. The fast approach of sine die (Capitol speak for last day) also means schedules will be even more tentative than usual, as deals get cut and chamber leaders try to squeeze in debate and procedural steps wherever they can. Read more Stay up-to-date with Colorado Politics by signing up for our weekly newsletter, The Spot.

Title: Cloudflare cuts 1,100 jobs despite record revenue, says AI made roles obsolete
URL: https://indianexpress.com/article/technology/tech-news-technology/cloudflare-cuts-1100-jobs-despite-record-revenue-says-ai-made-roles-obsolete-10681297/
Time Published: 2026-05-09T11:41:18Z
Full Content:
Cloudflare recently announced one of the biggest workforce cuts in its history, saying advances in artificial intelligence have made around 1,100 jobs obsolete even as the company reported record quarterly revenue growth. The internet infrastructure and cybersecurity company said it is reducing its workforce by nearly 20 per cent, affecting employees across multiple teams and regions. The layoffs come despite Cloudflare posting its highest-ever quarterly revenue of $639.8 million in the first quarter of 2026, marking a 34 per cent year-on-year increase. Cloudflare CEO and co-founder Matthew Prince described the decision as part of a larger shift toward AI-driven operations rather than a traditional cost-cutting exercise. Speaking during the company’s earnings call, Prince said the company had seen dramatic productivity improvements after aggressively adopting AI tools internally over the past few months. According to Prince, Cloudflare employees are now using AI across engineering, human resources, finance, marketing, and other departments. The company says its use of AI has increased by more than 600 per cent in the last three months alone. Prince claimed some employees became “two, 10, even 100 times more productive” with AI assistance, comparing the transition to moving from a manual screwdriver to an electric one. One major focus has been software development. Cloudflare said nearly its entire research and development division now uses AI-assisted coding tools. The company also revealed that all AI-generated code deployed into its products is reviewed by autonomous AI agents before release. As a result, the company believes fewer support roles are needed around highly productive AI-powered teams. Prince suggested that certain traditional support functions may no longer play the same role inside modern tech companies as AI becomes more capable. The layoffs mark the first large-scale workforce reduction in Cloudflare’s 16-year history. Chief Financial Officer Thomas Seifert said the cuts affect nearly every department except sales teams tied directly to revenue generation. Despite the job cuts, Cloudflare insists it still plans to continue hiring in the future. Prince said employees who effectively use AI tools are becoming significantly more valuable and productive, adding that the company could eventually grow its total workforce again by 2027. The announcement reflects a broader trend across the tech industry, where companies including Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon are increasingly linking workforce reductions to AI adoption while continuing to report strong revenues and major investments in artificial intelligence infrastructure. Cloudflare ended the quarter with around 5,500 employees before the layoffs took effect.
--------------------------------------------------

Title: What Is ROI? Complete Guide to Return on Investment and How to Calculate It
URL: https://bitcoinfoundation.org/news/altcoins/what-is-roi-complete-guide-to-return-on-investment-and-how-to-calculate-it/
Time Published: 2026-05-09T10:08:27Z
Full Content:
What is ROI? ROI is defined as “Return on Investment” — a financial indicator that represents the profit an investment has generated compared to the initial investment. ROI meaning in finance is tied to measuring how effectively capital generates profit across projects or investments. As a result, a high ROI figure means an investment has generated significant profit with relatively little capital. Since ROI figures represent profit as a percentage of the original investment, it’s easy to compare investments with different levels of risk. Businesses use ROI to assess whether investments in initiatives such as a new product launch or software purchases will be profitable. It allows them to determine whether capital is being allocated wisely. For example, if a company is deciding between investing in two types of products, ROI allows it to determine which option is likely to generate more revenue. ROI is particularly helpful when an asset includes considerable volatility, such as cryptocurrencies. Asset price movements can greatly influence profitability over a very short period. In addition, ROI helps businesses track whether capital allocation decisions are generating real results rather than simply wasting money. The main advantage of ROI is that it expresses profitability as a percentage, enabling the comparison of two disparate investments even if one is much larger or much smaller than another. In percentage terms, a $500 profit on a $5,000 investment is much better than on a $50,000 investment. The value of this pliability is that ROI, described in simple terms, can be defined as the value created per investment dollar, which enables the measure to be used to measure investment ROI in stock, crypto assets, advertising campaigns, and small businesses. Although ROI and profit are closely related, they are not the same. Profit shows the total amount earned after costs are deducted, while ROI measures how efficiently the investment produced that profit. For example, let’s say two companies each earn $10,000 in profit. However, one company invested $50,000 while the other invested $200,000. As a result, their ROIs will differ significantly. Therefore, the difference between ROI and profit is that profit represents the total earnings after accounting for business costs, whereas ROI measures how efficiently capital was used to generate that profit. In general terms, ROI is the amount of income received on an investment in relation to its cost. It is typically expressed as a percentage. ROI is: The ROI formula is normally written as: ROI = (Net Profit / Cost of Investment) × 100. This return on investment formula is widely used in stock trading, crypto investing, marketing, and real estate. This formula calculates a positive ROI for a profit and a negative ROI for a loss. When learning how to calculate roi, an investor must first determine total investment costs, including any fees, taxes, or operating costs. This percentage is the amount by which the final return exceeds the initial amount paid, divided by the initial amount, which provides a useful means of comparing investments against each other, independent of their initial purchase price. A basic ROI example can be seen in stock investing. If an investor buys shares worth $2,000 and later sells them for $2,400, the profit equals $400. Read Also: What Is Crypto Arbitrage Trading and How Does It Work? Complete Beginner’s Guide (2026) Using this formula ($400/$2000) x 100%, or 20%, it follows that the ROI is 20% in this example. Such a formula is a simple tool used by financial analysts to assess profitability before a more detailed investigation of the portfolio. More advanced ROI calculations may also include transaction fees, dividends, leverage, taxes, and a holding period for the investment. Annualized ROI is the standard measurement for comparing ROIs in most of the finance community. Such ROI analysis is especially useful in volatile markets such as the crypto market since short-term results can be substantial, but give a false impression of the longer-term results. It is also useful for comparing assets or portfolios that have very different levels of risk. To calculate ROI percentage correctly, the answer should always be multiplied by 100 after dividing the net profit by the cost of the investment; the result remains in decimal format. There is no universal standard for what makes a good ROI percentage, and acceptable returns depend on risk appetite, market conditions, and asset classes. In customary investments, the ROI is often compared to the rate of inflation, or to the performance of a benchmark index of the market at large. One of the clearest return on investment examples comes from the stock market. An investor in the stock market may buy a stock worth $5000, then later sell it for $6500, yielding a return on investment. The profit is $1500, before tax and brokerage charges. The standard formula determines that the ROI equals 30 percent. Shareholders can use ROI in conjunction with dividend income and annualized returns to establish whether their stock investment under- or outperformed market indexes in a given timeframe. Crypto ROI can also be affected by volatility; for example, if Bitcoin is bought for $10,000 and sold for $15,000, then the return would be 50%, excluding trading fees or taxes. $DOGE doesn't need headlines. As $BTC holds $82K, risk appetite is rotating and liquidity is back.​DOGE usually lags, then spikes fast. Quants just increased its weight after a 25% monthly index ROI. This is systematic, not retail.​Clock is ticking. ⏳​#DOGE #BTC #Crypto pic.twitter.com/8VAovMZdrJ Since the price of most cryptocurrencies is highly volatile in the short term, the ROI is periodically calculated in real-time instead of at the closing of a position, which enables the assessment of the short-term profitability or risk of a crypto portfolio. A normal ROI computation related to real estate would include rental income, maintenance costs, taxes, and appreciation of property. A $200k rental property with $18k annual income after expenses would yield a 9% annual ROI. Annualized rate of return (ROR) is of great importance to property investors, as real estate performance is highly correlated to the market cycles, cost of financing, and operating expenses over long periods, as well as mortgage rates and renovation costs. In advertising, marketing ROI is calculated by determining whether the revenue generated by a campaign exceeds the cost of the campaign. For example, if a business spends $10,000 on digital ads and generates $25,000 in attributable revenue, the ROI equals 150%. As such, due to modern web analytics solutions, which allow businesses to track their conversions, their customer acquisition costs, and the overall success of their campaigns to an extraordinary degree, ROI is one of the most important metrics for marketing budget optimization. At small organizations, the ROI of operational spending is typically tied to an investment that will cut operational costs. For example, if a business spends $15,000 on a workflow automation tool and that reduces its annual operating spend by $30,000, the investment has a positive ROI. Small business owners often use ROI analysis in allocating limited funds. In competitive industries, ROI analysis is useful to identify which investments lead to increasing profitability and which investments fail to yield measurable benefits. In finance, return on investment (ROI) is an indicator used to measure the efficiency of an investment or to compare the efficiencies of different investments in stocks/bonds, mutual funds, and corporate projects. Read Also: Top 5 Crypto Trading Setups for Quick Gains: Boost Short-Term Performance Banks also use ROI figures when assessing whether to acquire a business, to assess growth opportunities, or to decide how best to distribute funds over the long term. The ratio is one of the most widely used measures of profitability worldwide due to its breadth and simplicity. Marketing ROI calculates the revenue from a campaign as a return on the investment in the campaign. MROI enables companies to measure the effectiveness of advertising on search engines, email, influencers, and display ads. The modern marketer calculating ROI relies heavily on attribution models and customer lifetime value, which can help clear up whether marketing activity translates into long-term growth for the business. Social media ROI is the return on investment from using social media platforms such as Instagram, TikTok, X, and LinkedIn. Businesses will measure social media ROI by tracking engagement metrics, conversion rates, leads, and customer acquisition costs. In contrast to measuring the ROI of customary advertising, measuring the ROI of social media doesn’t only mean measuring direct revenues. Increasingly, indirect returns are becoming important for digital-first companies, for example, measuring brand awareness, audience growth, or customer retention. In property investing, real estate ROI is calculated from rental income, operating expenses, borrowing costs, and appreciation. Real estate investors use ROI to evaluate residential, commercial, and short-term rental properties. Real estate investing deals with large transactions and maintenance costs, and therefore, the effective return on investment must be compared to the interest rate and the condition of the local housing market. Crypto ROI can be useful in unstable market conditions where returns can vary widely in daily or even hourly rounds of trading. It can be used to assess token performance, staking rewards, and portfolio performance, and is used by traders and long-term investors. #Bitcoin 4-Year ROI as of 2026-05-08Current Price: $79,876Price 4 Years Ago: $34,0264-Year ROI: 135% $BTC #ROI #BitcoinChartBot pic.twitter.com/Gf6Q0M26xT Due to the changing nature of digital asset markets, cryptocurrency return on investment (ROI) is calculated in real time. Fees, liquidity, and market risk are other factors that investors consider when determining if potential profits are worth the volatility of crypto assets. The main advantage of ROI is that it shows how profitable a capital investment is. Investors use investment ROI to see whether a stock, crypto asset, or business project gives a good return on their investment compared to their cost, and if another investment would give better returns. Since ROI is a percentage, it is easier for analysts to make comparisons between the relative returns of disparate investments, which proves especially useful in fast-moving financial environments. Companies and individual investors also use ROI to inform an investment decision. Knowing the ROI helps them know if they are wasting money on a bad investment, or if another has more potential for growth. In corporate finance, ROI analysis is often used in evaluating business expansion, the purchase of new software, or the upgrade of a computer network, to see if the proposed expenditure will bring a satisfactory return given its risk. A further advantage of ROI is its unit-free nature, meaning that it can be used to compare assets across sectors and asset classes, such as comparing the profitability of equities, real estate, or even digital assets. This flexibility is why ROI explained in finance education is frequently presented as a universal benchmark for comparing relative returns regardless of investment size or structure. For businesses, ROI isn’t just about profit. Companies calculate the ROI of their marketing spend, their new hires, or their new computer systems to determine whether the operating costs are yielding concrete benefits. Read Also: Essential Crypto Trading Metrics: Top 10 Indicators for Crypto Traders ROI in business can help identify departments, projects, or campaigns that are underperforming and causing financial losses. In a competitive industry, analytics-based decisions can maximize profit and output. An important drawback of ROI is that it does not consider how long it took to earn the returns. Thus, two investments can have the same percentage return on investment, but one may have taken a few months, and the other may have taken several years to realize the profit. Crypto Equities Just Rallied 36% in Two Weeks. Here's What We Do Now.Crypto equities are splitting into winners and losers, and the divide is widening fast. One corner of the market has returned +346% since June 2024. Another is likely to keep underperforming Bitcoin… pic.twitter.com/AFtAo3l8PS It is therefore common to use ROI alongside annualized values such as compound annual growth rate (CAGR) or internal rate of return (IRR), to determine capital growth throughout the holding period. Colloquially, this measure is also called return on investment (ROI), but this measure does not take into account the risk associated with the capital investment. Cryptocurrencies and speculative stocks can have a high ROI, but they carry a high risk. Researchers and analytics experts increasingly argue that ROI becomes misleading when risk exposure is excluded from performance analysis. “Measurement of advertising ROI is extraordinarily noisy. Even large-scale experiments often struggle to detect the true financial impact of marketing campaigns.” — Randall Lewis and Justin Rao, researchers in advertising effectiveness and econometrics research Consequently, professional investors rarely rely solely on an ROI analysis, and would typically consider a range of other factors (including risk, market conditions, and liquidity) before committing capital to any risky investment. ROI calculations can be gamed by omitting indirect costs or selectively reporting data, for example, by focusing marketing budgets on revenue growth while undervaluing operational or customer acquisition costs. Modern ROI measurement increasingly focuses on incrementality rather than simple attribution models. “Most platforms are designed to credit themselves. Meta reports conversions. Google reports conversions. When both claim the same results, marketers are left reconciling numbers that do not match reality.” — Mike Lim, Managing Director at Insight Out, 2026 Additionally, accounting variances may make comparisons across firms or industries less relevant, so analysts may focus as much on the assumptions behind the reported ROI percentage as the percentage itself. Another limitation of ROI is that it does not show when cash flows come into the business. An investment might offer a good ROI, but still be problematic for liquidity if revenues or payments for goods and services are delayed or depend on periodic payments. For this reason, it is common to consider ROI along with the cash flow statement and balance sheet. Profitability and ROI do not disclose all the financial and operational details about a business. Profitability is closely related to ROI and ROE; however, it is not the same. ROI (return on investment) is the return on an investment, or how much profit was generated by an investment, while ROE (return on equity) is how much profit was generated by shareholders’ equity. Hence, ROE is often used to gauge the efficiency of a company or to compare the efficiency of a number of publicly listed companies. ROI can be used to assess investments, marketing campaigns, or a business project. Return on Assets indicates how successful a company’s total assets are at generating earnings. Whereas ROI is usually calculated for a particular investment or project, ROA is a measure of performance at the operational level, across an entire company. Investors frequently use the ROA ratio to evaluate banks, manufacturers, or other asset-heavy companies where equipment and infrastructure are necessary to generate a profit. ROI is better for evaluating the profitability of individual investments. Since IRR takes into account the time value of an investment’s cash flows, it can be considered superior to ROI. However, they are different measurements of different things, as ROI is a simple percentage return, and IRR is the estimated annualized growth rate of the investment. This is of particular interest in venture capital, private equity, and real estate projects where the cash flows may occur at numerous times over a period of years. The IRR method is frequently used in long-term financial projection and capital budgeting. CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) represents the rate of return on an investment over a given period of time, assuming an investment has been compounding at a consistent rate during that time. CAGR is not affected by volatility. For example, an asset may have a high total ROI over a five-year time period, but has had meaningful price fluctuations during those five years. CAGR allows an investor to understand the expected annualized return instead of just the total return. While ROI can be used to measure profitability, the payback period is usually used to measure the speed at which the initial investment is repaid, rather than simply the dollar amount. For companies considering new equipment or expansion, these measures are sometimes used alongside one another to weigh the risk of a shorter payback period versus a lower ROI over the economic life of the investment. Reducing costs is one of the most effective ways to increase return on investment. Investors or companies typically focus on transaction fees, operating costs, finance expenses, and maintenance costs to increase the net profit. In competitive markets, small reductions in operating costs can translate to high returns over the life of the investment, especially when margins are narrow or revenues vary. For a business to increase ROI, revenues must grow faster than the total costs. This can be achieved by increasing price, retaining existing customers, converting more customers, or entering new markets. Dividends, appreciation of long-term assets, or improved allocation inside a portfolio are three sources of investor ROI, but steady earnings growth generally has a greater impact than speculation. Diversification, which minimizes risk and eases better long-term returns, is usually achieved when capital is spread across stocks, hybrids, and fixed income products as opposed to one asset class, like stocks or cash. Read Also: Bitcoin Price Perspectives in May 2026: Will BTC Surge to $100,000? This approach is regularly utilized in constructing modern portfolios since it reduces drawdowns and protects overall returns in the event of market declines. In volatile asset classes like crypto, it also improves long-term investment roi. ROI should be monitored to determine whether an investment or business decision is continuing to produce good returns over time. Things change quickly, and market circumstances, operating expenses, and customer trends can all shift suddenly. Many companies use dashboards and analytics software to track their ROI percentage in real-time. Regular reporting allows investors and companies to react more quickly to falling markets, rising costs, or poorly performing assets. Missing unexpected costs or indirect costs is also a frequent mistake in ROI calculations. Transaction fees, taxes, maintenance costs, software subscriptions, or financing can all eat into the actual profitability of a benefit. This pattern is often seen in investing (e.g., real estate investing and crypto trading), and hidden operating costs may have a larger impact than realized if all costs are not considered. Errors in the formulas used to calculate performance measures can affect the quality of an analysis and the specific investment decision. Some investors calculate gross performance numbers that do not include operating expenses, taxes, or transaction costs. For this reason, understanding how to calculate return on investment correctly is an important part of making comparisons between assets or business projects. Short-term high returns may not be indicative of long-term future returns, and could be higher due to higher volatility, liquidity risk, or growth rates that may not be sustainable. Businesses or investors can also apply longer-term measures, such as CAGR or internal rate of return (IRR), to prevent overestimating temporary improvements in return for equity invested. Taxes and inflation can shave the real return on investment for an investor. This is especially true when nominal interest rates or consumer prices are high. An investment that seems profitable may, in real terms, be unprofitable. This is especially problematic with international investing and investing in cryptocurrencies, where the tax treatment varies between countries, and ignoring it could considerably inflate an ROI analysis for these investments. Most modern ROI calculator sites offer annualized return calculations and irregular cash flows, or investment situations where periodic deposits are made by a user or compounded. However, XIRR calculators continue to gain traction, particularly with long-term investors, who appreciate being able to account for multiple investments made at various times rather than at once. More financial services for portfolio analysis and mutual fund analysis are also adopting XIRR calculators. However, Excel is a popular tool for investors to analyze an investment’s ROI. Functions such as XIRR, IRR, CAGR, etc. from Excel can be used in these situations when there are not evenly distributed inflows/outflows, as well as when the holding period is very long. Analysts often use multiple ROI models, forecasting, budgeting, and sensitivity analysis in Excel. These models deliver greater flexibility than the common free online calculators and are often used by businesses and institutional investors. More companies now use analytical systems that link sales and marketing data with operational data, presenting them through dashboards to assess campaign effectiveness, customer acquisition cost, and total profitability. Modern ROI tracking software often interfaces with customer relationship management (CRM) systems, ad networks, and accounting systems so that companies have less error-prone and more timely information about measurable ROI from departments or campaigns. Portfolio trackers like Empower, Kubera, Sharesight, and Seeking Alpha help track multiple accounts among different assets like stocks, ETFs, and crypto to see all dividends and portfolio allocations in one place. Current platforms generally offer advanced analytics, including fee calculators, tax reporting, index or peer-based benchmarks, and retirement projections. Automated applications for tracking portfolios across multiple asset classes will replace manual spreadsheets and provide data on long-term investment roi. Yes. A negative result means the investment lost money because the costs exceeded the final return. Title: Memory stocks are the tech sector's hottest trade. Here's why.
URL: https://www.businessinsider.com/memory-stocks-rally-chips-tech-stock-market-investing-sndk-mu-2026-5
Time Published: 2026-05-09T09:40:01Z
Full Content:
Memory chip stocks have emerged as the next big wave of the AI trade and investors are all in. AI is driving up demand for memory, sending the stocks of the companies that make memory chips soaring and helping to lead the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite to fresh records. The Roundhill Memory ETF, which trades under the ticker, DRAM, demonstrated the group's meteoric rise. DRAM has gained around 88% since it began trading just over one month ago on April 2. Roundhill CEO, Dave Mazza, told Business Insider that the firm created the fund because there was a gap in the market for a memory-focused ETF. "Memory chips are the most supply-constrained layer of the AI infrastructure buildout, but investors had no efficient way to access the theme," he explained. The ETF is made up of several global memory chip company stocks including US names like Sandisk and Micron along with Korean companies like SK hynix and Samsung, Japan's Kioxia Holdings, as well as Taiwan-based Nanya Technology and Winbond Electronics. The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq Composite are trading at record highs, largely shrugging off war in Iran and elevated oil prices— and memory stocks are leading the charge, seeing some of the largest year-to-date gains in the index. Sandisk has been the top S&P 500 performer so far in 2026, gaining a whopping 558%. Seagate has risen 172% while Western Digital and Micron more than doubled, gaining 156% and 137%, respectively. AI is evolving from training to inference, fueling a surge in demand for memory as the tech relies on data which is stored on memory chips, especially in its inference stage. Ruben Dalfovo, an investment strategist at Saxo, said this "shift makes memory more strategic, because inference needs speed, bandwidth and power efficiency, not just raw compute." Micron explained that memory and storage components are "the heartbeat of AI," describing the components as "serving as its cognitive backbone, enabling innovation, accelerating performance, and redefining their traditional role from commodity to strategic asset." AI is driven memory demand sky high to the point where supply can't keep up. "The memory market is at an unprecedented inflexion point, with demand materially outpacing supply," IDC, a tech market intelligence firm, said. During the AI boom, memory chipmakers have prioritized making high bandwidth memory solutions over making consumer electronic components, lifting prices across the board. "AI servers and enterprise environments require far more memory per system than consumer devices, so the AI build-out is pulling a disproportionate share of global capacity and creating shortages, as suppliers prioritize orders from hyperscalers and OEMs building AI servers," IDC explained. The firm cautioned that this memory cycle is different than previous tech waves as AI is reshaping the market itself. "This is not just a cyclical shortage driven by a mismatch in supply and demand, but a potentially permanent, strategic reallocation of the world's silicon wafer capacity," they said. "Memory chips are the AI bottleneck," Roundhill's Dave Mazza told Business Insider. "Demand from hyperscalers is non-discretionary, supply is physically constrained with new fabs taking three to five years to come online, and Micron's entire 2026 HBM allocation is sold out under fixed pricing agreements," he added. While demand outpaces supply, memory chip prices have surged. In fact, memory cost headwinds were a trend in Big Tech earnings. Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the jump in the company's spending, was due in part to higher memory chip costs. Apple also called out high memory costs as a headwind. The AI-driven memory price rises are also weighing on consumers, making laptops and other devices more expensive. While pricy memory chips are a headwind for the companies and consumers that rely on the component, it's good news for the chipmakers who produce them. As the gains pile up, Wall Street analysts are still bullish. In a recent note on Sandisk, Bank of America flagged that demand is outpacing supply, lifted its price target for the stock. Bernstein analyst Mark Newman pointed out that the average selling price for Sandisk's products is "sky high" in a bullish note lifting the firm's target. And it's not just Sandisk. Micron, Seagate, and Western Digital are also seeing price target hikes on demand strength. Mazza explained that the current memory stock rally is a "re-rating story," saying that "Memory has been a cyclical industry for decades and the market still values these companies through that lens. What is being underappreciated is that when 65% of your revenue comes from hyperscalers on multi-year committed contracts, you become a contracted infrastructure supplier, not a cyclical." "That re-classification has started, but we believe it likely has further to run," the CEO said. Correction: May 9 — An earlier version of this story stated the incorrect year to date gain for Sandisk stock. The figure has been updated. May 08, 2026 16:30 ET | Source: NVIDIA NVIDIA SANTA CLARA, Calif., May 08, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- NVIDIA today announced that it has named to its board of directors Suzanne Nora Johnson, effective July 13, 2026. Ms. Nora Johnson is the former Vice Chairman of The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., where she spent two decades in various leadership roles, including serving as Chair of the Global Markets Institute, Head of Global Research and Head of Global Healthcare. She is expected to join the board’s Audit Committee on the effective date of her appointment. “Suzanne is an extraordinary leader whose career spans finance, technology, healthcare and public policy,” said Jensen Huang, founder and CEO of NVIDIA. “Her experience guiding global companies, together with her leadership at the forefront of education and philanthropy, will be an invaluable asset to NVIDIA’s board. We are honored to welcome her and look forward to her insight as we build the future in the age of AI.” Nora Johnson, 68, holds a J.D. from Harvard Law School and a B.A. from the University of Southern California. She serves on the board of directors of Pfizer Inc., where she is chair of the Audit Committee, a member of the Executive Committee and a member of the Regulatory & Compliance Committee. She also recently stepped down as the Board Chair of Intuit Inc., where she had served on the board of directors since 2007. Effective on July 13, 2026, NVIDIA’s board expands to 11 members.
